assessor_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT — real_world_framing_006

The retrospective comparison presented as justification for expansion has three significant methodological problems. First, the default rate comparison (3.1% auto-approved vs. 3.4% manual) is confounded by selection: auto-approved loans were pre-screened by the model's own scoring, while manual loans include the model's non-eligible population. This comparison does not measure model quality on comparable applicants. Second, both proposed expansions — raising the loan ceiling to $50,000 and lowering the FICO threshold to 620 — move into populations entirely absent from the model's training data. The model has no validated performance evidence for either expanded regime. Third, the plan bypasses shadow testing or champion-challenger validation, meaning the first performance signal from the expanded regimes would be actual credit losses.

The model's 14-month production track record within its current scope is positive evidence of in-distribution performance. This does not transfer to the proposed expanded scopes without prospective validation.

Verdict: The expansion plan as proposed is not appropriately justified. Prospective shadow testing of both expanded populations is required before extending auto-approval authority.
